data.title: Workday Is Maintained at Neutral by DA Davidson
Savers and the wealthy count the costs of UK tax-raising budget
data.description:
Key points:
  • Budget tax hikes target pensions, savers and investors
  • Concerns over disincentivising saving
  • Stock and bond markets rally after Reeves speaks

By Naomi Rovnick, Kirstin Ridley and Iain Withers

British money managers hit out at a raft of tax increases on pensions, savers and investors in Wednesday's budget, saying it risked damaging people's appetite to save.

Finance minister Rachel Reeves unveiled more than 26 billion pounds ($34.4 billion) of fresh tax hikes to prop up government finances, with several falling on pension savers and the wealthy.

The broad sweep would take total public sector receipts to 1.5 trillion pounds and 42.4% of GDP by 2030-31 and will raise the tax burden to a post-war high, official forecasters said, in large part by dragging more people into higher tax brackets.

Stock and government bonds reacted positively to the budget, however, with the initial reading that Reeves' budget had bolstered UK fiscal credibility.

STEALTH TAX RISES AND SYMBOLIC MEASURES

"Chancellor Rachel Reeves opted for stealth tax rises and symbolic measures targeting wealth and property, while avoiding the inflationary consequences of new business taxes," said Tim Service, an investment manager at Jupiter Asset Management .

Among the measures, tax benefits will be cut for salary-sacrificed pension contributions and taxes hiked on dividends, property and savings income, raising a combined 7 billion pounds according to Office for Budget Responsibility forecasts.

The tax-free limit on cash savings accounts known as 'ISAs' will also be cut to 12,000 pounds from 20,000 pounds for under-65s from 2027, part of a government effort to encourage savers to invest in Britain's stock markets instead.

Shares in money managers rose on hopes they could benefit from savers switching to investments to hold on to tax benefits, although some cautioned that Britain had a less established investing culture than the United States.

St James Place's shares rose 5.3%, Aberdeen's by 2.6% and AJ Bell's by 3.2%. IG Group soared 10.3%.

Other new taxes - including one on homes worth more than 2 million pounds - would take time to bed in to see their full effect, wealth firms said.

"I think what we'll see is a lot of postponed investment decisions," said Jim Brown at Blick Rothenberg, a tax and accounting firm.

PENSION SAVERS HIT

One of the most criticised measures by investors was the reduction of tax benefits for salary-sacrificed pensions from 2029, which official forecasters estimated would raise a bigger-than-expected 4.7 billion pounds in 2029/30.

Under salary sacrifice schemes, individuals can put some of their pre-tax earnings into their pension. Both employer and employee avoid having to pay tax on it.

That perk will now be removed for any contributions above 2,000 pounds a year, a move that hits employers of the biggest earners and the highest paid hardest, since they put more into their pensions.

"Any step that disincentivises people saving is unhelpful," said Simeon Willis, chief investment officer at pensions consultancy XPS.

The lag before the policy is implemented could cause problems for the government, said former pensions minister and Lane Clark & Peacock partner Steve Webb.

"Three years to prepare is incredible and they just won't get the money ... Unpicking salary-sacrifice is messy," Webb said, noting some top-paid staff could opt for pay cuts and have their pension fully paid by their employer instead.

($1 = 0.7568 pounds)

data.title: TSMC Sues Ex-Executive, Now at Intel. Trade Secrets Are Why. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Tae Kim

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is concerned that Intel could gain access to its chip-making secrets.

On Tuesday, TSMC filed a lawsuit in Taiwan's Intellectual Property and Commercial Court against Wei-Jen Lo, a former executive who now works at Intel, saying there is a high chance he could divulge critical information to his new employer.

Intel has tried to compete with TSMC, which currently dominates the global market for making advanced chips, in the so-called foundry business of producing semiconductors for third parties.

Lo couldn't be reached. Intel didn't immediately respond to a request for comment or for contact information for Lo.

Lo joined TSMC in 2004, the company said. The executive was promoted to senior vice president in 2014 and contributed to the foundry's research and development of advanced technologies. He retired from TSMC on July 27.

TSMC alleges Lo held meetings with staff of the company's R&D unit to learn about technologies under development after he was transferred to the Corporate Strategy Development department in March 2024. At the time, he wasn't responsible for supervising those employees, the chip maker said.

TSMC said Lo signed noncompete and nondisclosure agreements.

As Lo has joined Intel after retiring from TSMC: "There is a high probability that Lo uses, leaks, discloses, delivers, or transfers TSMC's trade secrets and confidential information to Intel, thus making legal actions (including claiming damages for breach of contract) necessary," TSMC said.

Write to Tae Kim at tae.kim@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Royale Energy, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Royale Energy, Inc., a company engaged in the exploration and production of oil and natural gas, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of 2025. The report highlights significant financial improvements and operational developments despite facing challenges in production volumes and commodity prices.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenues: $1.39 million, a decrease of 21.5% compared to the same period in 2024, primarily due to lower oil and natural gas production volumes and lower oil commodity prices.
  • Loss From Operations: $(1.41) million, an improvement from a loss of $(1.89) million in the same period in 2024, mainly due to lower lease operating, lease impairment, and credit loss expenses.
  • Net Loss: $(1.65) million, improved from a net loss of $(2.07) million in the same period in 2024, attributed to cost reductions and settlements of accounts payable.
  • Net Loss available to common stock: $(1.65) million, improved from $(2.72) million in the same period in 2024, reflecting the impact of reduced expenses and settlements.
  • Basic and Diluted Net Loss Per Share: $(0.02), improved from $(0.04) in the same period in 2024, due to a decrease in net loss and an increase in the number of shares outstanding.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The company's revenues are derived from the sale of crude oil, condensate, natural gas liquids (NGLs), and natural gas. For the nine months ended September 30, 2025, oil and condensate sales were $1,187,236, natural gas sales were $182,925, and NGL sales were $2,758.
  • Sales Units: The net sales volume of oil and condensate for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, was approximately 18,592 barrels, while the net sales volume of natural gas was approximately 84,898 Mcf.
  • Geographical Performance: The company experienced weather-related issues in its Texas Jameson field, which impacted production volumes. Additionally, some California natural gas wells were offline due to mandatory pipeline inspections by Pacific Gas and Electric.
  • Operational Efficiency: Oil and natural gas lease operating expenses decreased by 34.4% for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, compared to the same period in 2024, due to lower workover-related costs and equipment repairs.
  • Future Outlook: Management plans to increase revenues by participating with industry partners in drilling wells in the Permian basin and continuing to drill and workover wells in the Texas Jameson field. The company anticipates that expected increases in revenue, along with reduced capital expenditures for drilling, should allow it to meet its liquidity needs through the remainder of 2025.

SEC Filing: Royale Energy, Inc. [ ROYL ] - 10-Q - Nov. 26, 2025

data.title: Fitch Affirms Chandler USD No. 80, AZ's IDR at 'AA+' and School Improvement Bonds at 'AAA'
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Austin-26 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed the following Chandler Unified School District No. 80, AZ ratings:

--Issuer Default Rating (IDR) at 'AA+;

--Approximately $260 million in Fitch-rated school improvement bonds at 'AAA'.

The Rating Outlook is Stable.

The 'AA+' IDR incorporates a 'aa' financial resilience assessment based on Fitch's expectation for maintenance of unrestricted general fund reserves of 15%-20% of spending, given the district's 'low midrange' level of budgetary flexibility.

Also supporting the rating is the district's rapid population growth trend and 'strongest' demographic and economic level metrics compared to Fitch's local government ratings portfolio. Additionally, the district's long-term liability burden is assessed as 'strong' reflecting relatively low to moderate direct debt and Fitch-adjusted net pension liabilities to personal income and governmental resources. The rating reflects a -1 notch model deviation from the Model Implied Rating (MIR), which improved to the low end of the 'AAA' range (see below for more information).

The 'AAA' rating on the school improvement bonds is based on amendments to Arizona Revised Statutes that provide unlimited tax bondholders with a statutory lien on ad valorem taxes of cities, towns, counties, school districts, community college districts and various special districts in the state. Fitch believes the statute provides bondholders with a substantial preferential right in a bankruptcy proceeding, warranting a bond rating two notches higher than the entity's IDR. The statutory lien applies only to ad valorem tax revenues and applies both to bonds previously issued and to be issued in the future. The legislation did not affect Fitch's IDR for the district.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--A decline in available general fund reserve levels sustained below 15% of spending, which would lower Fitch's assessment of financial resilience to below 'aa';

--Material increases in liabilities beyond the scope of the recently authorized $271.5 million bond package;

--A prolonged trend of average daily membership (ADM) losses, resulting in sustained, material declines to state funding.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--A sustained increase in, and expectation for maintenance of, unrestricted general fund reserves above 20% of spending which would increase the financial resilience assessment to 'aaa';

--Sustained improvement in the district's demographic and economic metrics.

SECURITY

The bonds are general obligations of the district payable from an unlimited ad valorem tax levied against all taxable property in the district, backed by a statutory lien through state legislation.

Fitch’s Local Government Rating Model

The Local Government Rating Model generates Model Implied Ratings, which communicate the issuer's credit quality relative to Fitch's local government rating portfolio. (The Model Implied Rating will be the Issuer Default Rating except in certain circumstances explained in the applicable criteria.) The Model Implied Rating is expressed via a numerical value calibrated to Fitch's long-term rating scale that ranges from 10.0 or higher (AAA), 9.0 (AA+), 8.0 (AA), and so forth down to 1.0 (BBB- and below).

Model Implied Ratings reflect the combination of issuer-specific metrics and assessments to generate a Metric Profile and a structured framework to account for Additional Analytical Factors not captured in the Metric Profile that can either mitigate or exacerbate credit risks. Additional Analytical Factors are reflected in notching from the Metric Profile and are capped at +/-3 notches.

Ratings Headroom & Positioning

Chandler Unified School District No. 80 Model Implied Rating: 'AAA' (Numerical Value: 10.08)

-- Metric Profile: 'AAA' (Numerical Value: 10.08)

-- Net Additional Analytical Factor Notching: 0.0

Chandler Unified School District No. 80's Model Implied Rating is 'AAA'. The associated numerical value of 10.08 is at the lower end of the range for a 'AAA' rating.

Key Rating Drivers Deviations from Model Implied Rating

A minus 1-notch temporary model deviation was applied to Chandler Unified School District No. 80's Model Implied Rating (MIR) as the MIR improved modestly to 10.08 which is at the lower end of the 10.0 to 11.0 range for an 'AAA' rating. The MIR improved from 9.89, or the upper end of the range for a 'AA+' rating, due to modest and potentially temporary improvement in the district's unemployment rate relative to the U.S. unemployment rate. Furthermore, the MIR is likely to revert to the 'AA+' range as the district issues new debt associated with a $271.5 million bond authorization approved by voters this year.

Financial Profile

Financial Resilience - 'aa'

Chandler Unified School District No. 80's financial resilience is driven by the combination of its 'Low' revenue control assessment and 'High' expenditure control assessment, culminating in a 'Low Midrange' budgetary flexibility assessment.

-- Revenue control assessment: Low

-- Expenditure control assessment: High

-- Budgetary flexibility assessment: Low Midrange

-- Minimum fund balance for current financial resilience assessment: >=15.0%

-- Current year fund balance to expenditure ratio: 19.9% Analyst Input (vs. 23.1% 2024 Actual)

-- Lowest fund balance to expenditure ratio for the fiscal-year period 2020-2024: 19.9% Analyst Input (vs. 20.5% actual in fiscal 2020)

Revenue Volatility - 'Strongest'

Chandler Unified School District No. 80's weakest historic three-year revenue performance is neutral to the Model Implied Rating.

The revenue volatility metric is an estimate of potential revenue volatility based on the issuer's historical experience relative to the median for the Fitch-rated local government portfolio. The metric helps to differentiate issuers by the scale of revenue loss that would have to be addressed through revenue raising, cost controls or utilization of reserves through economic cycles.

-- Lowest three-year revenue performance (based on revenues dating back to 2005): 4.9% increase for the three-year period ending fiscal 2012

-- Median issuer decline: -4.3% (2024)

Analyst Inputs to the Model

Analyst inputs to the model reflect metric adjustments to account for historical data anomalies, forward-looking performance shifts, or non-recurring events that may otherwise skew the time series.

The analyst input for the five-year low unrestricted general fund reserve balance reflects Fitch's expectation that unrestricted reserves will be maintained between 15% and 20% of spending consistent with a 'aa' financial resilience assessment. This is based on the district's historical results of reserves holding at these levels, anticipated capital pay-go needs, and the potential use of reserves to help absorb the financial impact of the Maricopa County $13.9 million property tax refund liability arising from the Qasimyar v. Maricopa County class action lawsuit.

Demographic and Economic Strength

Population Trend - 'Strongest'

Based on the median of 10-year annual percentage change in population, Chandler Unified School District No. 80's population trend is assessed as 'Strongest'.

Population trend: 1.9% 2023 median of 10-year annual percentage change in population (83rd percentile)

Unemployment, Educational Attainment and MHI Level - 'Strongest'

The overall strength of Chandler Unified School District No. 80's demographic and economic level indicators (unemployment rate, educational attainment, median household income [MHI]) in 2024 are assessed as 'Strongest' on a composite basis, performing at the 82nd percentile of Fitch's local government rating portfolio. This is due to relatively strong education attainment levels, median-issuer indexed adjusted MHI and unemployment rate.

-- Unemployment rate as a percentage of national rate: 75.0% 2024 (83rd percentile), relative to the national rate of 4.0%

-- Percent of population with a bachelor's degree or higher: 47.2% (2023) (84th percentile)

-- MHI as a percent of the portfolio median: 131.5% (2023) (78th percentile)

Economic Concentration and Population Size - 'Strongest'

Chandler Unified School District No. 80's population in 2023 was of sufficient size and the economy was sufficiently diversified to qualify for Fitch's highest overall size/diversification category.

The composite metric acts asymmetrically, with most issuers (above the 15th percentile for each metric) sufficiently diversified to minimize risks associated with small population and economic concentration. Downward effects of the metric on the Metric Profile are most pronounced for the least economically diverse issuers (in the 5th percentile for the metric or lower). The economic concentration percentage shown below is defined as the sum of the absolute deviation of the percentage of personal income by major economic sectors relative to the U.S. distribution.

-- Population size: 287,287 (2023) (above the 15th percentile)

-- Economic concentration: 17.6% (2024) (above the 15th percentile)

An adjustment was made for the use of 2023 unemployment data in the absence of final 2024 census data.

Long-Term Liability Burden

Long-Term Liability Burden - 'Strong'

Chandler Unified School District No. 80's long-term liability metrics remain moderately strong across each of the three dimensions: liabilities to personal income, liabilities to governmental revenue, and carrying costs to governmental expenditures. The long-term liability composite metric in 2024 is at the 62nd percentile, indicating a somewhat lower liability burden relative to the Fitch's local government rating portfolio.

-- Liabilities to personal income: 4.0% Analyst Input (64th percentile) (vs. 4.2% 2024 Actual)

-- Liabilities to governmental revenue: 157.5% Analyst Input (60th percentile) (vs. 162.9% 2024 Actual)

-- Carrying costs to governmental expenditures: 13.1% Analyst Input (62nd percentile) (vs. 12.8% 2024 Actual)

Analyst Inputs to the Model

Analyst inputs to the model reflect metric adjustments to account for historical data anomalies, forward-looking performance shifts, or nonrecurring events that may otherwise skew the time series.

Analyst adjustments for the long-term liability metrics were made to account for the amortization of scheduled principal through fiscal end 2025 and for the increase in annual debt service following the issuance of new debt in fiscal 2024.

PROFILE

Chandler Unified School District No. 80, located in Maricopa County (IDR AAA/Stable Outlook), is the second largest school district in the county and the state. It had a student body of roughly 43,000 in fiscal 2024. The district's service area is affluent and continues to experience economic growth and diversification, most notably, a new Intel Corp campus that has generated roughly 3,000 new jobs. The district operates in an 80 square mile service area that includes portions of the cities of Chandler, Queen Creek and Gilbert. Construction continues the southern side of the district, while the northern portion is essentially built out and comprised of an older demographic base

Sources of Information

In addition to sources of information identified in Fitch's applicable criteria specified below, this action was informed by data from DIVER by Solve.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: Turkey cenbank chief says disinflation takes time, on track
data.description:
Turkey's central bank chief said on Wednesday that disinflation is a "long term process" in which progress has been made, and the bank will do all that it can to ensure inflation declines in line with its targets.

Governor Fatih Karahan, in an interview broadcast by the bank with journalists and an economist, said demand conditions are in line with the disinflation process, and he repeated the bank intends to reach single-digit inflation.

Short- and medium-term indicators suggest disinflation continues, he said. Turkish inflation eased a bit more than expected in October to 32.87% annually and to 2.55% monthly.

data.title: Fitch Downgrades Deerfield Episcopal Retirement Community, NC to 'BBB+'; Outlook Stable
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Chicago-26 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has downgraded to 'BBB+' from 'A-' the series 2016 revenue bonds issued by North Carolina Medical Care Commission on behalf of Deerfield Episcopal Retirement Community (Deerfield). In addition, Fitch has downgraded Deerfield's Issuer Default Rating (IDR) to 'BBB+' from 'A-'. Fitch has assigned a 'BBB+' rating to the series 2026A, 2026B-1, 2026B-2, 2026B-3 and 2026B-4 revenue bonds issued by North Carolina Medical Care Commission on behalf of Deerfield.

The Rating Outlook is Stable.

The downgrade to 'BBB+' coincides with Deerfield's plan to move forward with its large capital project, which has been contemplated over the last four or five years.

Deerfield has maintained its strong, consistent balance sheet and profitability metrics that are sufficient or strong relative to its rating. The community's desirable location and attractive pricing result in robust demand from prospective residents in North Carolina and outside the state. About 75% of Deerfield's units are independent living units (ILUs), and ILU occupancy is nearly 100% each fiscal year.

Like many other life plan communities (LPCs), Deerfield has experienced rising costs in labor, supplies and other areas. Management has implemented strategies to offset these increases, primarily targeting labor costs but also implementing a rate increase.

The series 2026 bonds, $11.4 million equity contribution and $50 million of bank bonds will fund the $348 million construction of 165 new independent living units (ILUs) and various other projects on the campus, a portion of accrued interest and cost of issuance. The series 2026B-1, 2026B-2, 2026B-3 and 2026B-4 bonds are anticipated to be repaid with entrance fees from the sale of the new ILUs in FY 2028/2029. The bonds are expected to sell the week of Dec. 17.

The Stable Outlook reflects that Deerfield's historical strong operations and successful execution of past projects.

SECURITY

The bonds are secured by pledged assets, which include gross receipts and a first-mortgage lien.

KEY RATING DRIVERS Revenue Defensibility - 'a'

Robust Demand Continues to Drive Strong Occupancy Despite Rate Increase

Deerfield has very strong ILU occupancy numbers, averaging 97.2% over the last five fiscal years. Assisted living unit (ALU) and skilled nursing (SNF) bed occupancy has been softer but still sufficient at five-year averages of 87% and 88%, respectively. Fitch believes Deerfield will continue to implement strategies to improve SNF occupancy, including possible direct admits. ALUs and SNF beds only make up about 25% of total units, so lower Health Center occupancy should not have a material effect on the community's overall operational performance.

Deerfield has a waitlist of roughly 1,600 prospective residents, with waiting periods for an apartment as long as five years. High demand is partially due to the community's desirable location in Asheville, NC, which is a national retirement and tourist destination. Management reports that 42% of current residents originate from the primary market area (PMA) (Buncombe and Henderson Counties), 28% from North Carolina, and 30% from outside the state. Deerfield's strong draw from outside its PMA partially insulates it from any significant changes in local real estate trends.

Minimal competition suggests ILU occupancy will continue to be strong, especially given the community's sizeable waitlist.

Deerfield continues to implement rate increases above historical levels due to the economic headwinds the industry is facing. Fitch believes that despite the sizeable rate increase, continued strong demand for ILUs supports revenue defensibility and Deerfield's plan for strategic growth.

Operating Risk - 'bbb'

Entrance Fee Cash Flows Remain Strong

Deerfield's core operating performance is on par with a midrange assessment, whereas its entrance fee cash flow metrics align with a strong assessment. The community's five-year averages for operating ratio, net operating margin (NOM) and NOMA-adjusted (NOMA) are 101.5%, negative 9.3% and 23.4%, respectively. These levels are in line with Fitch's expectations for a Type-A LPC operating model that focuses on entrance fee turnover with discounted monthly fees. Deerfield has implemented annual rate increases of 5% or greater over the past three years. Fitch expects Deerfield's operational performance will remain strong as it moves forward with the campus expansion.

Capital-related metrics remain sufficient for the rating. Capex-to-depreciation has averaged roughly 105% over the last five years, and average age of plant is about 14 years. Deerfield has long held strategic plans to expand the campus but placed the project on hold in 2022 due to rising construction expenses. Its current plans will help accommodate the growing demand, reflected in a waitlist of over 1,600 prospective residents. Deerfield successfully executed previous expansion projects, and Fitch expects that it will also be successful with the current plan.

Revenue-only MADS was 1.9x as of unaudited 2025, which is in line with historical performance and a midrange assessment. Fitch believes this demonstrates management's ability to direct operations efficiently without overly relying on cash flow from entrance fees. The community's debt burden remains manageable relative to its operating profile, where MADS was 10.4% of total unaudited 2025 revenues and debt-to-net available averaged 2.8x over the last five years. Both metrics are adequate for the rating.

Financial Profile - 'bbb'

Sizeable Debt Issuance Limits Financial Profile

Deerfield's unrestricted cash and investments totaled about $146 million at unaudited FYE 2025, representing a very strong 362% of adjusted debt and 1,357 days cash on hand (DCOH). MADS coverage, including entrance fees, had a strong five-year average of 3.6x, well in excess of its current covenant requirement of 1.2x. Based on information provided to Fitch, Deerfield's pro forma MADS coverage would dip to 1.4x in unaudited FY 2025, including the pro forma debt.

Fitch believes Deerfield will continue to benefit from strong demand due to its desirable location and reputation. Cash flow from entrance fees will continue to accrue from existing units, and balance sheet metrics will improve over time. Fitch expects cash-to-adjusted debt and MADS coverage to drop but slowly recover to levels consistent with the rating by year four of the forward-looking scenario, which factors in Fitch's standard portfolio stress. Fitch expects Deerfield's leverage metrics to be weak in the early years of the base case and improve after the opening of the new project.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--An unexpected significant decline in demand, such that ILU occupancy is expected to remain below 93% for a sustained period.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Given the likelihood of the expansion project and the project's scope, an upgrade is not likely during the Outlook period;

--Continued liquidity growth supported by stable, strong cash flow, resulting in cash-to-adjusted debt closer to 120% even in the stress case;

--Although unlikely for a Type-A community, core operations significantly improve, where NOM rises to at least 13% and operating ratio falls below roughly 93%.

PROFILE

Deerfield is a Type-A LPC located on 125 acres in Asheville, NC. It consists of 379 ILUs, 62 ALUs and 62 SNF beds. Beginning in 1999, Deerfield began phasing out its fee-for-service agreements and switching to a lifecare contract model. The community currently offers three separate residency agreements: a standard plan (fully amortizing over the first 48 months), a 90% refundable plan, and a 50% refundable plan. As of September 2025, the majority of Deerfield's contracts were standard. Total operating revenues in unaudited fiscal 2025 were $51 million.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit https://www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

data.title: UK faces emerging gas supply risks as demand falls slowly, energy operator warns
data.description:
Britain's energy operator said on Wednesday that gas will remain critical for energy security into the next decade and warned of potential shortfalls on the coldest days.

The National Energy System Operator said in its first gas security of supply assessment after being established last year that overall gas demand will decline as Britain decarbonises, but peak-day demand will fall more slowly, leaving risks if progress on cutting emissions stalls or if major infrastructure fails.

The report, which looks ahead to winters in 2030/31 and 2035/36, said Britain will continue to draw on a diverse mix of sources - including domestic production, Norwegian imports, liquefied natural gas, European pipelines, storage and biomethane - though output from the UK Continental Shelf will keep shrinking.

NESO said mitigating measures may be needed to maintain resilience, including reducing peak-day demand, maximising existing infrastructure and developing new supply options.

"Gas remains a critical component of Britain's energy security - keeping homes warm, powering industry and supporting electricity generation during periods of peak demand and low renewable output," said Glenn Bryn-Jacobsen, director of energy systems and resilience at National Gas.

"In considering potential solutions, it is essential to look at both the gas supply landscape and the investment required in network infrastructure."

data.title: REG - Playtech PLC - Impact of the UK Autumn Budget
data.description:
RNS Number : 1731J Playtech PLC 26 November 2025  

26 November 2025

Playtech plc

("Playtech" or the "Group")

Impact of the UK Autumn Budget

Playtech plc notes the UK Chancellor of the Exchequer's delivery today of the 2025 Autumn Budget and, within that, the increase in Remote Gaming Duty (effective from April 2026) from 21% to 40% and a new Remote Betting Rate within General Betting Duty (effective from April 2027) of 25% (versus a current rate of 15%).

Playtech currently estimates an impact on 2026 Group Adjusted EBITDA of up to high-teens millions of euros before mitigation. However, given the Group's geographic diversity across regulated markets and strong performance and prospects outside of the UK, Playtech remains comfortable that it can meet market expectations for the full year 2026.

- ENDS -

Playtech is the gambling industry's leading technology company delivering business intelligence driven gambling software, services, content and platform technology across the industry's most popular product verticals, including, casino, live casino, sports betting, bingo and poker. It is the pioneer of omni-channel gambling technology through its integrated platform technology, Playtech ONE. Playtech ONE delivers data driven marketing expertise, single wallet functionality, CRM and responsible gambling solutions across one single platform across product verticals and across retail and online.

Playtech partners with and invests in the leading brands in regulated and newly regulated markets to deliver its data-driven gambling technology across the online and retail value chain. data.title: PLATTS: 100--Senate confirms Ho Nieh nomination to US Nuclear Regulatory Commission
data.description: (Platts)--19Nov2025/110 pm EST/1810 GMT **Vote was 66-32 **Nieh pledged to maintain NRC regulatory independence **Weaver nominated, hearing pending for commissioner The US Senate voted Nov. 19 to confirm the nomination of Ho Nieh to serve on the US Nuclear Regulatory Commission. The vote was 66-32. Nieh served for more than 20 years at the NRC as well as in senior roles in industry and nuclear safety organizations. He is senior vice president for regulatory affairs at Southern Nuclear but was most recently on a long-term assignment to the industry's independent Institute for Nuclear Power Operations. The Senate Committee on Environment and Public Works favorably reported Nieh to the Senate late last month. Democratic committee members warned Nieh during his Oct. 8 confirmation hearing that infiltration of NRC by staff from the Department of Government Efficiency and Department of Energy was ongoing under the Trump administration, posing a fundamental threat to the agency's independence. "If confirmed, I will 100% stay committed to the independent safety position of the NRC," Nieh said during the hearing. Nieh's confirmation makes him the fourth commissioner, leaving one vacancy. Douglas Weaver was nominated Nov. 5 to serve as a commissioner. Weaver worked for 19 years for NRC earlier in his career and is a former Westinghouse regulatory affairs executive. Weaver's nomination hearing has not yet been scheduled. If confirmed by the Senate, he would serve through June 2026, completing a term vacated in July by former Commissioner Annie Caputo, a Republican. NRC Chairman David Wright, a Republican, was confirmed for a second five-year term on July 28. Caputo announced that day that she would resign after Wright was sworn in to focus on her family. Commissioners Bradley Crowell and Matthew Marzano, both Democrats, were appointed by former US President Joe Biden. — Steven Dolley, steven.dolley@spglobal.com-- Edited by Giselle Rodriguez, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: PLATTS: 100--Belarus gives green light to construction of Ostravets-3 nuclear reactor
data.description: (Platts)--14Nov2025/238 pm EST/1938 GMT **Russia commits to support development of Belarus' industry **Second nuclear plant construction remains on the agenda The Belarusian government approved the construction of a third 1,200-MW VVER-1200 reactor at the existing two-unit Ostravets plant during a meeting Nov. 14, Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko said in a statement on his website the same day. During the meeting, Lukashenko said the country had reaped economic and ecological benefits from the construction of the Ostravets plant and noted that the nuclear industry was undergoing expansion globally. "Many Western politicians were trying to focus exclusively on renewable energy," Lukashenko said. "Today, they're already talking about the advantages of building a nuclear power plant." Lukashenko added that Russia has committed to further support the development of the nuclear industry in Belarus. Belarus had been looking into either building Ostravets-3 or an entirely new nuclear plant, Lukashenko said, adding that the country had opted for the third unit due to the already developed infrastructure at the Ostravets site. "And the soil [at Ostravets] has already been thoroughly examined, so no further research is needed," Lukashenko said. Building new units at a new site in the eastern part of the country also offers certain advantages and has not been discounted, Lukashenko said, adding that construction of a second plant could "transform the eastern region [of Belarus] from a backwater into a highly developed territory." Belarus will survey potential sites for a new nuclear plant in the Mogilev region of the country, where electricity consumption is forecast to rise in the coming years, Lukashenko said. Ostravets-1 was connected to the grid on June 10, 2021, becoming the first VVER-1200 put into commercial operation outside of Russia. Ostravets-2 followed on Nov. 1, 2023. — Vladislav Vorotnikov, ci.support@spglobal.com-- Edited by Johanna Leo, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Brazil's federal government posts $6.8 billion primary surplus in October
data.description:
Brazil's federal government recorded a primary budget surplus of 36.5 billion reais ($6.83 billion) in October, official data showed on Wednesday.

The monthly result, which is usually positive in October, came in below the 41 billion reais surplus posted in the same month last year, according to figures from the treasury.

Despite a 4.5% real increase in net revenue, supported by record tax collection for the period, total expenditures rose at a faster 9.2% pace, driven by higher health outlays and social security benefits.

Over the 12 months through October, the central government's primary deficit reached 41.9 billion reais, equivalent to 0.35% of gross domestic product.

The government has set a target of balancing the primary budget this year, with a tolerance band of 0.25% of GDP above or below the goal.

Economic officials have repeatedly said the target will be met.

($1 = 5.3445 reais)

data.title: An Amazon Delivery Drone Hit a Cable Line in Texas. What to Know. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

As battery, electronics, and artificial intelligence technologies advance, the skies above the U.S. will become busier and busier. That will create some problems for companies and regulators.

The latest example comes via a Reuters report about an Amazon.com delivery drone. The Federal Aviation is investigating a midday Nov. 18 incident, when a MK30 drone struck a wire line in Waco, Texas, the agency said in an emailed statement to Barron's.

Amazon said the damaged line has been fixed.

"After completing a delivery, our drone clipped a thin, overhead internet cable then performed a Safe Contingent Landing, as designed," the company said in an emailed statement. "There were no injuries or widespread internet service outages. We've paid for the cable line's repair for the customer and have apologized for the inconvenience this caused them."

Amazon launched its MK30 drone in late 2024. A more capable model than Amazon's previous drones, the MK30 is designed to deliver packages weighing five pounds or less in under 60 minutes.

Amazon doesn't disclose drone delivery figures in its quarterly results. The company didn't immediately respond to Barron's request for delivery numbers.

The drone involved in the Nov. 18 incident underwent FAA testing, according to Amazon. MK30 drones fly defined routes at altitudes between 115 feet and 400 feet. The drones are monitored, but have automated navigation systems which feature the ability to make landings for safety reasons — like what happened last week.

The incident appears relatively minor. Amazon stock was up 0.9% in midday trading, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average were up 1% and 0.9%, respectively.

It's a reminder that drone and electric vertical takeoff and landing, or eVTOL, technologies aren't going away. The FAA has registered more than 450,000 commercial drones across the U.S.

eVTOL makers Joby Aviation and Archer Aviation, meanwhile, are on the cusp of starting commercial services. (eVTOLs are treated like aircraft and not drones, and are subject to much higher regulatory hurdles from the FAA.)

Investors will have to be ready to react to news about how new technology is affecting the world. Hopefully, the internet cable collision is as serious as things get.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

data.title: Herbalife jumps after Argus Research upgrades to 'buy'
data.description:
** Shares of nutritional products firm Herbalife <HLF.N> rise about 11% to $13.11 in afternoon trading

** Argus Research upgrades HLF to "buy" from "hold" on renewed confidence in its turnaround and growth prospects

** Expects mid-single-digit growth in 2025, driven by new products like HL/Skin and MultiBurn, following three years of decline

** Shares of HLF have surged 80% in six months, outperforming the Consumer Staples ETF , which has fallen 0.9%

** "We warn that HLF shares are likely to be volatile and thus suitable only for risk-tolerant investors," Argus Research says

** 2 of 4 brokerages rate the stock "buy" or higher, 1 "hold" and 1 "sell" or lower; their median PT is $11

** Including session's moves, HLF stock is up ~96.2% YTD
